coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous electrochemical determination of persistent petrogenic organic pollutants based on AgNPs synthesized using carbon dots derived from mushroom
coredata.dc:creator: Thulasinathan, Boobalan
Ganesan, Veerapandi
Manickam, Pandiaraj
Kumar, Ponnuchamy
Govarthanan, Muthusamy
Chinnathambi, Sekar
Alagarsamy, Arun
coredata.dc:description: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are highly carcinogenic substances and accumulate in water bodies through various industries. Due to their harmful effects on humans, it is very important to monitor PAHs in various water resources. In the present work, we report an electrochemical sensor based on silver nanoparticles synthesized using mushroom-derived carbon dots for the simultaneous determination of anthracene and naphthalene, for the first time. Pleurotus species mushroom was used to synthesize the carbon dots (C-dots) via the hydrothermal method and these C-dots were used as a reducing agent for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The synthesized AgNPs have been characterized through UV–Visible and FTIR spectroscopy, DLS, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM, and HR-TEM. Well-characterized AgNPs were used to modify glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) by the drop-casting method. Ag-NPs/GCE has shown strong electrochemical activity towards the oxidation of anthracene and naphthalene at well-separated potentials in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) at pH 7.0. The sensor exhibited a wide linear working range of 250 nM to 1.15 mM for anthracene and 500 nM to 842 μM for naphthalene with the corresponding lowest detection limits (LODs) of 112 nM and 383 nM respectively with extraordinary anti-interference ability against many possible interferents. The fabricated sensor showed high stability and reproducibility. The usefulness of the sensor for the monitoring of anthracene and naphthalene in a seashore soil sample has been demonstrated by the standard addition method. The sensor gave better results with a high recovery percentage indicating the first-ever device to detect two PAHs at the single electrode with the best analytical results.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous electrochemical determination of persistent petrogenic organic pollutants based on AgNPs synthesized using carbon dots derived from mushroom

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are highly carcinogenic substances and accumulate in water bodies through various industries. Due to their harmful effects on humans, it is very important to monitor PAHs in various water resources. In the present work, we report an electrochemical sensor based on silver nanoparticles synthesized using mushroom-derived carbon dots for the simultaneous determination of anthracene and naphthalene, for the first time. Pleurotus species mushroom was used to synthesize the carbon dots (C-dots) via the hydrothermal method and these C-dots were used as a reducing agent for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The synthesized AgNPs have been characterized through UV–Visible and FTIR spectroscopy, DLS, XRD, XPS, FE-SEM, and HR-TEM. Well-characterized AgNPs were used to modify glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) by the drop-casting method. Ag-NPs/GCE has shown strong electrochemical activity towards the oxidation of anthracene and naphthalene at well-separated potentials in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) at pH 7.0. The sensor exhibited a wide linear working range of 250 nM to 1.15 mM for anthracene and 500 nM to 842 μM for naphthalene with the corresponding lowest detection limits (LODs) of 112 nM and 383 nM respectively with extraordinary anti-interference ability against many possible interferents. The fabricated sensor showed high stability and reproducibility. The usefulness of the sensor for the monitoring of anthracene and naphthalene in a seashore soil sample has been demonstrated by the standard addition method. The sensor gave better results with a high recovery percentage indicating the first-ever device to detect two PAHs at the single electrode with the best analytical results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are compounds consisting of two or more fused benzene rings. These compounds have different structures like linear, angular, and cluster arrangements. PAHs have high melting and boiling points and are relatively insoluble in water; their water solubility decreases with the increasing number of aromatic rings. On the other hand, PAHs are partially or completely soluble in organic solvents and are lipophilic in nature (Lawal, 2017). PAHs are ubiquitous pollutants in the environmental systems, coming from natural and anthropogenic sources such as incomplete combustion of fossil fuel, burning coal, and wood, agro-waste burning, and power plant applications (Shen et al., 2013a; Ramesh et al., 2011). More accumulation of PAHs is found in the aquatic ecosystem, especially due to the anthropogenic activity inputs such as water, industrial wastes, urban runoff, seashore, and oil spills (Wu et al., 2011). These activities have harmful effects on the environment and human health, including respiratory, cardiovascular, and immunological diseases like genotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic effects (Idowu et al., 2019). PAHs occur in drinking water, municipal and industrial effluents, oil spillage or leakage at low concentrations, and sometimes even high-level detection limits. The electrochemical sensors are also used for monitoring PAHs contaminants in the aquatic environment, cured oil, and cooking oil.

The United States Environmental Protection Agency and World Health Organization promoted to formulate of policies and regulations to protect aquatic systems, primarily to safeguard the drinking water from environmental pollutants, and have identified more than 16 PAHs as national priorities list of pollutants (Idowu et al., 2019) which includes anthracene and naphthalene. According to European Union, the maximum permissible limit of anthracene and naphthalene in superficial water systems is 100 ng/L and 1200 ng/L respectively. Initially, PAHs have been only focused on their mutagenic and carcinogenic property (Boffetta et al., 1997; Rubin, 2001). But the recent studies focused on the effects of PAHs on the antagonism of hormonal functions and reproduction in humans (Drwal et al., 2019) and its teratogenicity (Abdel-Shafy and Mansour, 2016).

The most common methods used for the detection, identification, and quantification of PAHs, include gas chromatography (GC) (Tseng et al., 2014), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Arun et al., 2008; Arun and Eyini, 2011), and spectroscopy method (Du and Jing, 2011; Cai et al., 2008). Although the above techniques provide good accuracy in determining the PAHs, they are often time-consuming and expensive processes. The electrochemical method has been considered the most efficient technique in determining the environmental pollutants and specific electrochemical methodologies such as electrochemiluminescence (Li et al., 2010), electrochemical oxidation (Aćimović et al., 2017), and electrochemical immunoassay (Nsibande et al., 2019) have been developed. The electrochemical techniques offer more advantages like a simple instrument, facile operation, more accuracy with fast response, highly sensitive, short analytical time, and more economical process (Vyskocil and Barek, 2011). The latest research activities with regard to electrochemical methods for the determination of PAHs from aqueous resources have been reported in very recent reviews (Comnea-Stancu et al., 2021; Munawar et al., 2020). Nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors have been reported for the determination of dopamine, glucose biosensor, and PAHs' electrochemical sensing, including copper nanoparticles (CuNPs), gold nanoparticles (Au-NPs), graphene, polymers, and organic/inorganic composites (German et al., 2011; Nsibande et al., 2019; Oztekin et al., 2012).

In particular, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been widely used as electrodes (Lee et al., 2021; Singh and Buttry, 2012), catalysts (Jiang et al., 2005; Anandhakumar et al., 2014), and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) materials (Panigrahi et al., 2006) due to its admirable chemical and physical properties. AgNPs have been used to make optical (McFarland and Van Duyne, 2003) sensors for the detection of ammonia (Dubas and Pimpan, 2008a), water pollutants (Prosposito et al., 2020), copper (II) ion (Kirubaharan et al., 2012), enzyme (Wei et al., 2008), and herbicide (Dubas and Pimpan, 2008b). Basic properties of the nanoparticles such as particle size (Seney et al., 2009), morphology, composition, crystallinity, surface properties, and stability play a vital role in the intrinsic properties of AgNPs (Panigrahi et al., 2006). Owing to that, various methods of synthesis were designed to develop the stable properties of AgNPs (Khatoon et al., 2018, 2017). In many cases, the chemical synthesis of AgNPs consumes high energy and nanoparticles size cannot be easily controlled because of their strong reducing ability. Moreover, chemically synthesized AgNPs easily aggregate using even weak stabilizers. To solve these issues, novel stabilizing and reducing agents are needed. Jin et al., 2015 reported that fluorescent CDs-derived AgNPs have enhanced reducing ability and at the same time act as stabilizing agents (Jin et al., 2015).

Mushroom-derived carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used as stabilizing and reducing agents in the applications of toxic metal ion detection (Venkateswarlu et al., 2018; Pacquiao et al., 2018; Boobalan et al., 2020), volatile organic compound (VOC) sensing (Pacquiao et al., 2018), enzyme sensing (Yang et al., 2017), visual detection of G-quadruplex (Kumari et al., 2018), imaging (Pacquiao et al., 2018), antibacterial and anticancer agents (Venkateswarlu et al., 2018; Boobalan et al., 2020). Carbon dots are widely used for sensing applications due to their size, good electron conductivity, and catalytic activity. The structure of carbon dots is very similar to that of graphene whereas carbon dots have sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. In addition to this, there are many hydroxyls, carboxyl, carbonyl, and amine groups at the edges of the carbon dots.

In this study, C-dots-derived silver nanoparticles (C-dots-AgNPs) have been used as electrode material for the simultaneous detection of anthracene and naphthalene. C-dots-AgNPs have been prepared to exploit the capability for direct reduction of Ag+ to metallic Ag by amine or phenol hydroxyl groups on the aromatic ring of C-dots (Shen et al., 2013a, 2013b). This has the advantage of avoiding the use of an additional reducing agent or external photoirradiation. The C-dots derived AgNPs based sensor developed in the present work is the first-ever sensor for the simultaneous determination of anthracene and naphthalene in an aqueous solution, which is of utmost importance in the environmental field.

Anthracene and naphthalene, with a maximum purity of 98 % as determined by High-Pressure Liquid Chromatography (HPLC), were purchased from Himedia (Mumbai, India) and used without further purification. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Other chemicals and solvents used in the experiments were purchased from Super Religare Laboratories (SRL, India), HiMedia (Mumbai, India), and Merck Chemicals, India. All required solutions were prepared with deionized water.

The fresh fruiting body of Pleurotus species was collected from wood decay in the Science Campus of Alagappa University, India. The fruiting body was washed with distilled water to remove the surface adherent materials. Pleurotus species (10 g) was crushed with the help of a mortar and pestle and dispensed in 100 mL of distilled water with 5 % sulfuric acid. 75 mL of the sample was transferred to a 100 mL hydrothermal autoclave with Teflon, heated at about 150–180 °C for 6 h in the oven, and cooled at room temperature. A dark brown liquid product was obtained, which indicates the formation of water-soluble C-dots. The solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm at 4 °C for 15 min. The centrifugation was repeated three times, filtered, and purified with a Nylon membrane (0.22 μm). 15 mL of the C-dots solution was then added to 100 mL AgNO3 (1 mM), and the mixture solution was stirred for 45 min in a magnetic stirrer. As a result, a dark brown product was obtained, and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C, repeating this process three times to remove the impurities (De and Karak, 2013). Then solvent in the final product was evaporated to obtain the desired C-dots-AgNPs, dried, and stored in a desiccator at room temperature.

The formation of C-dots-AgNPs was confirmed by using a UV − vis spectrophotometer (UV-1800, Shimadzu, Japan). Chemical group functionalities with the C-dots-AgNPs were identified by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis (FTIR- ALPHA II, BRUCKER, Germany) after mixing with KBr pellets. The crystallographic structure of the C-dots-AgNPs was characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (PAN analytical X-Pert PRO). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis was used for measuring the average particle size and zeta potential in a suspension of C-dots-AgNPs (Zetatrac, USA).

The surface morphology, uniformity, and microstructure of the C-dots-AgNPs were studied using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) on a JEM 2011, JEOL instrument with a CCD 4 k × 4 k camera (Ultra Scan 400SP, Gatan) and scanning electron microscope spectroscopy (SEM, FEI Quanta FEG 200). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to investigate the elemental composition and chemical state of the C-dots-AgNPs.

Glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) polished with 0.05 μm alumina powder slurry and cleaned with deionized water was used in the electrochemical measurements. Voltammetric analyses were performed on the CHI 609D workstation with the assistance of a three-electrode system using GCE as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) electrode as the reference electrode. C-dots-AgNPs (1 mg) were dispersed in 1 mL water individually and ultra-sonicated for 30 min at room temperature to disperse the nanoparticles homogeneously throughout the deionized water. Then,10 μL of the sample was drop cast on the polished surface of GCE, and it was dried at room temperature.

The optical properties of synthesized C-dots and C-dots-AgNPs were evaluated by UV–visible Spectroscopy in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm, and the results obtained are shown in Fig. 1A . A strong absorption peak is observed at 269 nm which indicates the presence of conjugated π → π⁎transition of CC bond and aromatic sp2 hybridized carbon of mushroom containing organic compounds like amino acid and some organic moieties (Boobalan et al., 2020). The shoulder absorbance peak that appeared at 237 nm is due to the transition of polyaromatic hydrocarbons in the C-dots' carbon core. Introducing AgNPs in C-dots results in a broad absorption peak at 449 nm due to the surface plasmon resonance phenomena of AgNPs. Furthermore, the C-dot characteristic peak at 269 nm is shifted to 449 nm, simultaneously the intensity has decreased, and the shoulder peak got disappeared. These results suggest that C-dots directly and hydrophobically interact with AgNPs so that the characteristic C-dots peak was shifted to a higher wavelength.

FT-IR spectra of the synthesized C-dots and C-dots-AgNPs are shown in Fig. 1B. The peak in the region of 3431 and 3323 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration peak of the OH functional group. The peaks at 2365 and 2094 cm−1 represented the CH stretching vibrations of aliphatic hydrocarbon. The peaks at 1571and 1632 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching vibration and the bending vibrations of NH occur at 1380 and 1455 cm−1. The occurrence peak at 1068 and 1089 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of CO. The above results confirm that OH, NH, and CO groups from amino acids, carbohydrates, and some organic moieties were present in the C-dot. All of these peaks are also present in C-dots-AgNPs, but the peaks are slightly shifted to the lower wavenumber, and the peak intensity has decreased due to the hydrogen interaction between the C-dots and AgNPs.

Fig. 1C shows the obtained powder XRD spectra of C-dots-AgNPs. The diffraction peaks at 38.23°, 44.42°, 64.44° and 77.39° correspond to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of fcc crystal structure with a lattice constant a = 4.085 Å and agree well with ICDD card no. 840713. The presence of a diffraction peak at 33.2° corresponds to the crystallized bioorganic compounds (Philip, 2009). This result confirms that the silver nanoparticles are presented in the composite.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements were made to identify the dominant elements and to identify the chemical valance states. Fig. 2A shows the survey scan spectra of Ag 3d, C1s, and O1s. The carbon peaks (Fig. 2B) observed at a binding energy of 283.34, 284.50 and 287.30 eV correspond to peaks of CC, C-O-C, and O-C=O, respectively. In Fig. 2C, the sharp peaks at the binding energy of 366 eV and 372 eV match well with Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2. The 3d splitting doublet is 6 eV, which indicates the metallic nature of silver. The peak at 531 eV in Fig. 2D reveals the presence of oxygen in the composite. These results confirm the carbon quantum dot and silver presented in the composite.

Scanning Electron microscopy (SEM) is used to analyze the surface morphology and uniformity of the synthesized C-dots and C-dots-AgNPs. Fig. 3A and B show that the C-dots are crystalized in spherical morphology. SEM image also showed the aggregation of the AgNPs presence in C-dots. Their shape was similar to the environmentally friendly quantum dots with near spherical shapes as reported by Khouloud Jlassi et al., 2020 (Jlassi et al., 2020). Meanwhile, the synthesized C-dots-AgNPs have formed in uniform sizes, and the yield was very high as revealed by these images.

The synthesized C-dots-AgNPs were characterized by using HR-TEM. Fig. 3C and D show uniform spherical particles and confirmed the presence of well-dispersed silver nanoparticles. The C-dots are hardly seen in the TEM image because of their low contrast, as previously reported for Pd-graphene QDs (Yan et al., 2012). The average diameter of the C-dots-AgNPs is 6.77 nm, which is lower than the functionalized metal/carbon nanocomposite when ascorbic acid was used as a reducing reagent (Han et al., 2009). Further, the HR-TEM image of C-dots-AgNPs shows clear lattice fringes of Ag 0.234 (Fig. 3D).

The hydrodynamic diameter of the C-dots-AgNPs measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS), presented in (Fig. S1), is in the range from 60 nm to 200 nm, with an average of 147 nm. Shen et al., 2013a, 2013b reported that the growth of particle size at 35 nm to 111 nm of Ag-NPs on C-dots nanocomposites (Shen et al., 2013a, 2013b), which are greater than the mushroom-derived C-dots-AgNPs. Fig. S2 shows the zeta potential at the surface of the C-dots-AgNPs indicating highly negative (−65 mV), compared to C-dots from enokitake mushroom (−69 mV). The highly negative zeta potential is due to mushroom-derived C-dots, which have several hydrophilic functional properties such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl (Boobalan et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2017b).

A cyclic voltammetry study was performed to understand the electrocatalytic behavior of the electrodes in 0.1 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at a scan rate of 50 mV/s ( Fig. 4A ). Bare GCE showed a redox peak for the oxidation and reduction of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. At the C-dots-AgNPs modified GCE a strong oxidation peak was obtained at a potential of 0.052 V which is due to the oxidation of Ag0 into Ag(I). The corresponding reduction of Ag(I) to Ag0 was also evidenced by the appeared reduction peak at −0.162 V. Moreover, the oxidation peak corresponding to Fe(II) to Fe(III) transition was obtained at a potential of 0.446 V and a small reduction peak at 0.176 V.

Further, the effect of scan rate on the modified electrode was studied by increasing the scan rate from 60 mV/s to 100 mV/s in steps of 10 mV/s at each stage. From the results (Fig. 4B), it was observed that both the anodic and cathodic peaks get increased with the increase in scan rate, and the existence of linearity was confirmed from the plot of the square root of scan rate versus peak potential. The plot yielded the linear regression equation of Ipc (μA) = 190.35ν1/2 (V/s)1/2–39.30 with the correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.995 indicating a diffusion-controlled electrode process at the interface. The slope value (0.53) obtained from the plot of logν versus log Ipa confirms the diffusion controlled redox process at the electrode. The electrochemical active surface of the electrodes was calculated by using the Randles-Sevcik equation which gave the values of 0.083 and 0.256 cm2 for bare and modified GCE. The regression equation obtained for the plot of logν vs. Epc was Epc (mV) = 0.128–0.05 logν (R2 = 0.981). The coefficient of charge transfer (α) was calculated by using the formula,
(3)
Slope = 2.303 RT / αnF
Where n is the number of electrons transferred and the rest symbols has their usual meanings. The value of αn was found to be 0.46. For diffusion-controlled process the theoretical value of α is 0.5. Hence, the value of n was calculated to be 0.925 which is close to 1 and it confirms the one electron transfer redox process.

Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to study the reaction kinetics at electrode-electrolyte interface. The Nyquist plots for bare GCE and C-dots-AgNPs modified GCEs showed a semi-circle portion which corresponds to electrode-electrolyte and a linear portion corresponds to the diffusion-controlled process. The Rct value of bare GCE was found to be high (1109 Ω/cm2) compared to that of C-dots-AgNPs/GCE (967.1 Ω/cm2) which confirms the faster electron exchange at its surface (Fig. 4C). The standard heterogenous rate constant of the reaction was calculated from EIS results by using the formula,

Where k0 is the heterogenous range constant, A is the surface area of the electrode, and the other constants have their usual meanings. The calculated values of k0 were 0.937*10−6 and 1.075*10−6. The higher value of k0 for the modified electrode confirmed the fast electron transfer rate than the bare electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric behaviours of anthracene and naphthalene
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the scan on the oxidations of anthracene and naphthalene was studied by increasing the scan rate from 100 mV/s to 500 mV/s at the potential window of 1.0 V to 2.0 V. Fig. 5B shows the CVs recorded in the presence of AN at various scan rates. From the results, it is observed that the anodic peak currents increased linearly with the increase of scan rate with a slight positive shift which indicates that the reaction is governed by a diffusion-controlled process. The plot of the square root of scan rate (ν1/2) versus anodic peak current showed a linear relationship with the regression equation of Ipa (μA) = 83.61ν1/2 mV1/2 + 30.06 (R2 = 0.992). Similarly, the effect scan rate on naphthalene was studied with the same experimental conditions. The plot of ν1/2 vs. Ipa yielded a linear plot according to the linear regression equation Ipa (μA) = 133.37ν1/2mV1/2 + 54.66, R2 = 0.993 indicating the diffusion-controlled process. Moreover, the slope values of 0.277 and 0.271 obtained from the corresponding plots of logν vs. log Ipa for AN and NA were found to be closer to the theoretical value of 0.5 for the diffusion-controlled process with the regression equations of log Ipa = 0.45logν - 3.97 (R2 = 0.983) for AN and log Ipa = 0.47logν - 3.74 (R2 = 0.996) for NA respectively. Further, the plot of logν vs. Epa yielded a regression straight line with the R2 value of 0.99. The slope of this plot was found to be 0.02 and by using this slope the value of αn has been calculated as2.11 and 0.64 and the number of electrons transferred at the electrode was found to be 4.22 and 1.28 which supports the electro-oxidation reactions of AN and NA at the electrode. Fig. 5A and B shows the square wave voltammograms recorded at bare GCE and C-dots-AgNPs/GCE in the absence (a, b) and in the presence of 100 μM AN and NA. Strong anodic peaks were obtained at both bare GCE and C-dots-AgNPs/GCE whereas no response were observed in the absence of AN and NA. Compared to bare GCE, the C-dots-AgNPs/GCE has improved transfer rate which resulted in higher peak current. This result is in well agreement with the CV results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective determinations of anthracene and naphthalene
ce:section.ce:para:
Selective determination of one analyte in the presence of the other analytes was performed using square wave voltammetry (SWV). Fig. 6A shows SWVs recorded for various concentrations of AN at C-dots-AgNPs/GCE in the presence of 50 μM of NA in 0.1 M LiClO4 in acetonitrile. The oxidation peak currents obtained for the oxidation of AN increased after each addition of AN and current vs. concentration exhibited a linear relationship over the range of 250 nM to 1.15 mM. In a similar experiment, the SWV responses of NA were recorded for concentrations between 500 nM to 842 μM NA in the presence of 50 μM of AN (Fig. 6C). Plot of concentrations of AN and NP versus and its corresponding oxidation peak currents showed liner responses towards the determinations of AN and NA. From the plot of concentration versus current (Fig. 6B and D), the values of the slope, intercept and standard deviation (SD) were calculated and the linear regression equations were deduced and are as follows:

Also, the lowest detection limit (LOD) of the sensor was calculated with the values of standard deviation and slope by using the formula of LOD = (3*Standard deviation)/Slope and it was found to be 112 nM for AN and 383 nM for NA respectively. The C-dots-AgNPs/GCE exhibited enhanced sensing performance towards AN and NA which could be due to improved electrocatalytic properties and higher electron transfer rate at the modified electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous determination of anthracene and naphthalene
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 6E shows the SWVs recorded for the simultaneous determination of AN and NA at C-dots-AgNPs/GCE in 0.1 M LiClO4 in acetonitrile. The oxidations of both AN and NA were found to increase simultaneously with the increasing concentrations over wide in the range of 500 nM to 990 μM for both AN and NA. The plot of AN and NA concentrations versus their oxidation peak currents (Fig. 6F) showed a linear relationship between them and the corresponding linear regression equations have been deduced as follows:

From this equation, the LOD of the electrode was calculated to be 110 nM and 383 nM. Moreover, this sensor could effectively be used for the simultaneous determination of AN and NA. It can be noticed that the oxidations of AN and NA taken place at well separated potentials and that there was no significant shift in their positions for the entire investigated ranges. This result is very remarkable. To the best of our knowledge, there is no reported sensor for the determination of AN and NA (Table 1 ).

The selectivity study of the modified GCE was studied by determining the AN and NA in the presence of 100-fold excess of some potential interferents. Initially, SWVs were recorded in the presence of 50 μM AN and NA at the C-dots AgNPs/GCE, the interferents (Fluorene, Benzene, Acenaphthalene, Na+, Cu2+, Pd2+, Fe2+, NO3−) with concentrations of 100-fold higher than the concentration of AN and NA were added individually and its effect on the oxidations of AN and NA at the electrode were recorded. It is clear from the results (Fig. 7A), there are no changes in the peak currents of AN and NA noted which confirms that the electrode has superior selectivity with the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of 9.37 % and 6 % for AN and NA respectively. Fig. 7B shows the CVs of 100 cycles recorded at C-dots-AgNPs/GCE in 0.1 M LiClO4 in acetonitrile containing 70 μM of AN and NA. A similar CV response was obtained after the 50th cycle with the 1st cycle and the oxidation current values of AN and NA was found to be 3.62 μA and 16.29 μA for the 1st cycle and 3.06 μA and 14.56 μA for the 100th cycle with the loss percentages of 85 % and 89.3 % for AN and NA respectively. Moreover, this result proved that the electrode doesn't undergo peeling of material from the surface and thus leads to no change in current responses. It is confirmed that the electrode could be used for determining the AN and NA for about 100 times respectively without major loss in the electrode responses.

The reproducibility of the electrode was studied by ten different C-dots-AgNPs modified electrodes which are prepared under identical conditions. CVs were recorded by using these electrodes in the presence of 70 μM NA and AN, respectively (Fig. 7C). All the ten modified GCEs showed almost the same responses with the RSD values of 3.0 % each for the oxidation currents of AN and NA. This evidenced the suitability of the electrode for the simultaneous determination of AN and NA with higher selectivity, excellent stability and better reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of AgNPs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the modified electrode was tested by determining the amounts of AN and NA in real seashore soil samples (Fig. 8A), and it was collected from Green Gate, V.O. Chidambaranar Port Trust, Tuticorin, India. The real soil sample was taken as such and without any pre-treatment, subsequently, dissolved in water and the resulting solution was used for the determination. As there are no oxidation responses corresponding to AN and NA at the electrode, the standard addition method was followed by adding known concentrations of AN and NA in the range of 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM, and 50 μM was added and the corresponding voltammograms were recorded. From the voltammograms obtained, the concentration of AN and NA were calculated and the results were given in the Table 2 . Furthermore, the recovery percentage and relative standard deviation (RSD) for this analysis were determined and found to be in the ranges of 99.0 % - 100.2 % and 0.18–0.84 for AN and 99.8 % - 101 % and 0.13–0.66 for NA. From these results, it was found that the electrode showed excellent recovery percentage with better RSD value). As a whole, these results suggested that the C-dots-AgNPs modified GCE could be a good platform for the simultaneous determination of AN and NA for both laboratory chemical and real samples.

To further explore the possibility of the developed sensor for the analysis of environmental samples, investigations have been carried out for the detection of AN and NA in seawater, crude oil, and reused cooking oil. Several researchers reported the presence of PAHs in crude oil and reused cooking oil (Hao et al., 2016; Monaghan et al., 2021; Polli et al., 2020). Here, SWVs were recorded in 0.1 M LiClO4 in the presence of crude oil, reused cooking oil, and seawater sample ( Fig. 8B-D). In the case of seawater and reused cooking oil samples, no oxidation responses were obtained. So, the standard addition method was followed by adding known concentrations of AN and NA in the range of 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM and 30 μM. The values of RSD and recovery percentages have been calculated and the results were given in Table S1-S3. In the case of crude oil, five oxidation peaks were obtained between the potentials of 1.4 V and 2.0 V. A broad peak was obtained at about 1.88 V corresponding to the oxidation potential of NA and the peak intensity increased with the increase in crude oil. Subsequently, known amounts of commercial NA (5 μM to 30 μM) were added to the same electrochemical cell by the standard addition method and new peak emerged at about 1.82 V and its intensity increased with increase in concentration. The observed difference in oxidation potential may be due to contaminated crude oil. The results suggests that the observed peak at ~1.88 V could be due to the oxidation of NA present in the crude oil. Earlier, Wang et al., 2015 studied crude oil by Gas Chromatography to determine the presence of PAHs in crude oil and reported that NA was the principal constituent (Emsbo-Mattingly and Litman, 2016; Jia and Batterman, 2010; Wang et al., 2015). Building upon these findings, we have attributed the oxidation peak at around 1.88 V to the presence of NA in crude oil in proximity with commercial NA's oxidation potential of 1.82 V The concentration of NA was calculated by extrapolating the current values obtained from the SWVs and extrapolating them in the linear plot (Fig. 6D). Fig. S3 shows the extrapolation method for the calculation of amount of NA present in the crude oil and the results were given in Table S2. Thus, the fabricated sensor applies to any environmental sample in which AN and NA can be reliably detected.

The advances in electrochemical rapid detection of water adulterants over the last decades and the developed sensors platform from lab to commercial purpose are still challenged due to affected serval factors. Future direction to achieve the integrated system of developed sensors for low-cost, accurate, rapid detection that could be monitoring water adulterant levels in environmental resources. In the present work, AgNPs were synthesized by using mushroom-derived carbon dots for the first time and their structural and morphological characteristics were studied. The UV, FTIR, XRD, and XPS studies confirm the formation of AgNPs from the mushroom-derived carbon dots. The GCEs were modified by a drop-casting method using the synthesized C-dots-AgNPs and their electrocatalytic behavior was studied by cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The results paved the idea for the usage of the electrode for electrochemical sensing applications. As a result, the electrode was successfully applied for the determination of PAHs such as anthracene and naphthalene. The modified electrode C-dots AgNPs/GCE showed superior catalytic activity towards the determination of anthracene and naphthalene with wide linear ranges and low LODs. Also, the electrode displayed excellent selectivity, stability and reproducibility and was applied for determining in real samples such as marine soil sample. Based on these results, it could be concluded that the electrode has to be used for the determination of anthracene and naphthalene simultaneously.
